factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies the industry as “ANZSIC code C2221NZ (‘Structural Steel Fabricating’)” and also references filtering by “ANZSIC 4-digit code ‘C2221’,” satisfying the requirement to use ANZSIC C2221/ANZSIC06 C2221 classification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes employment figures for multiple years within 2018–2024 (2018–2023) that fall within the 4,200–4,600 range, specifically ~4,500 (2021) and ~4,400 (2022).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports estimated annual revenue figures for 2023 (~NZD 1,550m) and 2024 (~NZD 1,500m), which fall within the NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range for the 2023–2024 period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states an overall production/domestic fabrication capacity of “120,000–150,000 tonnes annually,” including in the conclusions and validation sections.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists multiple major fabricators (e.g., Steel & Tube, Brightwater Engineering, Pacific Steel, George Grant Engineering) but does not provide any geographic locations (cities/regions) for them. No locations like Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, or Timaru are given for at least three fabricators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly lists HS codes 7207 through 7216 in the tariff section and provides a table summarizing tariff rates for each code, satisfying the requirement to specify HS codes 7207–7216 (or a relevant subset) for structural steel imports.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says most HS 7207–7216 categories are “effectively duty-free” and mentions some lines can be 5% (e.g., 7208/7213/7214 shown as 0–5%). However, it does not specifically frame this as the NZ “Normal Tariff” nor does it explicitly note “certain 7216 sections” carrying 5%.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states City Rail Link steel demand as “approximately 25,000 tonnes” and explicitly says structural vs reinforcing is not disaggregated. It does not provide the required approximate ranges of 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural for CRL.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the City Rail Link project is “targeting completion by late 2026,” which places completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Te Kaha’s steel quantities but gives figures like 4,800 tonnes of reinforcing steel and module weights; it does not state that the Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel is approximately 9,000 tonnes. The specific ~9,000-tonne roof steel figure is missing.
The response states that Te Kaha (One New Zealand Stadium), Christchurch has an opening scheduled for April 2026, which satisfies the requirement that it opens in 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a section on the “New Dunedin Hospital Redevelopment,” describing it as a large hospital complex with phased construction and noting that structural steel erection is planned and expected to consume significant tonnage, indicating it as a major project requiring substantial structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response describes a triangulation methodology but does not present conflicting market size figures and then reconcile them by attributing differences to scope definitions (e.g., narrow product fabrication vs broader construction metalworks/services). It also does not explicitly contrast alternative market boundary choices or explain how those boundaries change the market size numbers.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly defines the structural steel fabrication industry as excluding mills and distributors, but its “Top Five” list still includes Steel & Tube (primarily a distributor) and New Zealand Steel (a producer/mill) as top fabricators. This fails to consistently distinguish fabricators from distributors/mills when identifying top companies.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states that tariffs are generally zero/minimal and therefore “negligible,” and that margins depend more on global steel prices, FX, and freight. However, it does not explicitly explain the “price umbrella” mechanism (i.e., how tariffs create a protected domestic price floor that disappears when tariffs are low/zero).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides an actionable, step-by-step method to obtain class-level data by downloading Stats NZ datasets and filtering the industry code column by ANZSIC “C2221” in Excel/online filters to extract employment and business counts (and similarly for financial data).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes a general softening between 2020–2023 and mentions “post-2022” recovery, but it does not explicitly connect employment or revenue changes to construction-cycle timing (e.g., a 2023 peak following a mid-2022 slowdown). There is no clear causal timeline linking the reported employment/revenue trend inflection points to the construction cycle.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions an aggregate estimate ("30,000 to 40,000 tonnes") and cites a few projects with some tonnage figures, but it does not validate any stated fabrication capacity/throughput by explicitly aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects into a clear summed total tied to capacity. It also includes projects without tonnage and mixes reinforcing vs structural without a consistent aggregation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that Stats NZ does not publish fabrication tonnage and then proposes a triangulation method using SCNZ capacity/workload surveys, HS 7207–7216 trade statistics, and aggregation of project steel demand/disclosures to estimate tonnage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly says tariffs are “negligible” and that fabricators “focus more on global steel price volatility [and] foreign exchange risk,” rather than blaming margin pressure on NZ import tariffs or border protection. It does not attribute competitive challenges to tariffs; it downplays tariff impact.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: While many figures include units like NZD million, persons, tonnes, and %, the response does not consistently provide explicit timeframes and precision for all quantitative claims (e.g., several values are approximate with “~”, ranges like “0–5%”, and some project steel tonnages are stated without clear source-year context). It also includes some quantities without explicit units or clear measurement basis (e.g., “80+ skilled trades”, “spans of 175 meters” without tying to FY/year precision requirements).

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections that separately cover market size/growth (Section 2), tariffs (Section 3), projects (Section 4), and companies (Section 6). However, it does not include a distinct section addressing “risks and opportunities,” only brief mentions in conclusions, so not every requested dimension is given its own section.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes speculative and unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., “2024 figures are preliminary,” “Forecast,” and projected project steel demand ranges) and uses promotional language such as “accurate, highly detailed, and replicable.” These elements break a strictly professional market research tone requirement without speculation or unsupported forward claims.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes a structured year-by-year table for 2018–2024 listing estimated annual revenue, employment, and fabrication tonnage, along with business counts. This satisfies the requirement for historical employment/revenue/capacity data presented in a table format.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, technical register throughout, using structured headings, tables, and source citations. It does not use rhetorical questions or colloquial/informal phrasing (e.g., slang, conversational asides) that would be inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response contains substantial filler and unnecessary elaboration, such as lengthy “step-by-step data extraction” instructions, repeated methodology/triangulation sections, and multiple overview/conclusion paragraphs that add length without adding new substantive market facts.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites “Stats NZ Business Demography Statistics” and “Stats NZ Business Employment Data” as sources for employment figures, and includes a hyperlink to the 2024 Business Demography release.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple Stats NZ datasets (e.g., “Business Financial Data” and “Business Demography Statistics”) for revenue and employment, but it does not cite the Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent named Stats NZ economic survey specifically for revenue/sales figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites the New Zealand Customs “Working Tariff Document” as the authoritative tariff schedule source and provides a hyperlink to the official Customs tariff page when discussing HS 7207–7216 duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) as a source of fabrication tonnage capacity and workload/throughput estimates, including a hyperlink to SCNZ “Industry Updates.”

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response notes that “Mid-sized and smaller fabricators are mostly privately held, with limited public financial disclosure,” which satisfies the requirement to mention limited public financials for private fabricators.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides project-specific steel demand figures and hyperlinks for at least two major projects: City Rail Link (with a cited CRL Annual Review link) and Te Kaha (with a BESIX Watpac project announcement link). These constitute specific data sources tied to major infrastructure projects.
